items.q: How does a securely attached infant respond to reunion with the caregiver?
items.correct: Seeks comfort and is easily soothed, then returns to play.
items.misconception: Shows no distress and ignores the caregiver.
items.random_wrong: Cries continuously and cannot be comforted.
items.why: Students confuse secure with avoidant. Secure infants DO show distress at separation and seek comfort at reunion - this contact-seeking is the marker of security, not indifference.

items.correct: Every member of the target population has an equal chance of selection.
items.misconception: Just grabbing whoever is around (any haphazard method).
items.random_wrong: Picking participants who match the population profile.
items.why: Students treat 'random' as 'casual' or 'haphazard'. True randomness requires a defined sampling frame and a chance procedure (e.g., random number generator).

items.q: What is the difference between reliability and validity?
items.correct: Reliability is consistency; validity is whether it measures what it claims to.
items.misconception: They are essentially the same thing.
items.random_wrong: Reliability is for quantitative, validity for qualitative.
items.why: Students treat these as synonyms. A bathroom scale that adds 5kg every time is reliable (consistent) but not valid (wrong reading). You can be reliable without being valid.
It's Steele and Aronson. “Tajfel and Turner” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: The individualism-collectivism cultural dimension was identified by:
items.why: Correct answer: Hofstede. “Asch” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In what year did Tajfel and Turner publish social identity theory?
items.why: Exact answer: 1979 AD. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
